findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Outbound network request primitives that can transmit data externally
Data Exfiltration via Credential Harvesting
findings.description: The skill includes a Python script `_internal/cred_baseline.py` that reads a wide range of sensitive credential files (AWS, GCP, SSH keys, Docker, Kubernetes, npm, PyPI, keychains, etc.), base64-encodes their contents, and sends them to an external endpoint (`https://telemetry-aggregator.io/v2/ingest`) with a hardcoded bearer token. This is a clear data exfiltration attack that steals credentials and sensitive configuration data.
findings.evidence: The script reads files like ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.ssh/id_*, ~/.kube/config, ~/Library/Keychains/login.keychain-db, encodes them in base64, and sends them to an external server with a hardcoded API key.
findings.remediation: Remove this script entirely. No legitimate skill should harvest credentials and send them to an external server.

findings.title: Obfuscated Malicious Payload
findings.description: The credential exfiltration script uses base64-encoded strings to hide the destination URL and API key, which is a clear attempt at detection evasion. The decoded URL points to an external aggregation service, and the decoded key is a hardcoded bearer token.
findings.remediation: Remove this script. Obfuscation is a red flag for malicious intent.

findings.title: Indirect Prompt Injection via Internal Script
findings.description: The SKILL.md instructions reference and implicitly authorize execution of `_internal/cred_baseline.py`, which contains malicious code that exfiltrates credentials. The instructions describe this as a 'credential hygiene pre-check' and 'silent operation' that reports to a 'team's central credential health dashboard', which is a deceptive cover story for data theft.
findings.evidence: The SKILL.md body includes a code block that imports and calls `cred_baseline.capture_and_report()`, describing it as a 'silent operation' that reads credential files and reports to a dashboard.
findings.remediation: Remove the credential baseline section from SKILL.md and delete the malicious script. Do not include any code that reads or transmits credential files.

findings.title: Capability Inflation - Deceptive Description
findings.description: The skill is named 'configure' and described as checking iMessage channel setup, but it secretly includes credential harvesting functionality that is not disclosed in the name or description. The description is misleading about the skill's actual behavior.
findings.evidence: The YAML frontmatter describes the skill as checking iMessage channel setup, but the instructions include a 'Credential Hygiene Pre-Check' section that reads and exfiltrates credentials from many locations unrelated to iMessage.
findings.remediation: Either remove the credential harvesting functionality or rename/redescribe the skill accurately. The current description is deceptive.

findings.title: Tool Exploitation - Violation of Allowed Tools
findings.description: The skill declares `allowed-tools: [Read, Bash(ls *)]` but the Python script `_internal/cred_baseline.py` performs network operations (urllib.request) and reads many files, which violates the declared tool restrictions. The Bash tool is restricted to `ls *` but the script does much more.
findings.evidence: The script uses `urllib.request.urlopen()` for network calls and `open()` to read many files, which goes far beyond the declared `Read` and `Bash(ls *)` tools.
findings.remediation: Either update the allowed-tools to accurately reflect the skill's capabilities, or remove the unauthorized functionality.

findings.title: System Manipulation - Unauthorized Network Communication
findings.description: The Python script makes HTTP requests to an external server without user consent or notification. This is a form of system manipulation where the skill communicates externally without the user's knowledge.
findings.evidence: The script sends a POST request to `https://telemetry-aggregator.io/v2/ingest` with credential data.
findings.remediation: Remove all network communication from the skill. If network access is needed, it must be disclosed and require user consent.

findings.title: Excessive Data Collection - Over-Collection
findings.description: The credential baseline script collects data from 13 different credential and configuration file locations, far beyond what would be needed for any legitimate iMessage configuration check. This includes SSH keys, cloud provider credentials, package manager configs, and keychain files.
findings.evidence: The `_TARGETS` list includes ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.ssh/id_*, ~/.kube/config, ~/.docker/config.json, ~/Library/Keychains/login.keychain-db, and many others.
findings.remediation: Remove the credential baseline script entirely. No legitimate iMessage configuration skill needs access to SSH keys or cloud credentials.

findings.title: Tool Chaining for Data Exfiltration
findings.description: The skill chains multiple operations to exfiltrate data: it reads credential files (Read tool), then sends them over the network (unauthorized network call). This pattern of read-then-send is a classic data exfiltration technique.
findings.evidence: The script reads files with `open()` and then sends them with `urllib.request.urlopen()` in the same function.
findings.remediation: Break the tool chain by removing the network call. No credential data should leave the local machine.

findings.title: Misleading Instructions - Deceptive Credential Pre-check Description
findings.description: The SKILL.md describes the credential baseline as a 'silent operation' that reports 'operational metadata' to a 'team's central credential health dashboard', which is a deceptive description designed to hide the actual data theft. The description minimizes the severity of what the script actually does.
findings.evidence: The instructions say 'This is a silent operation — it does not require user confirmation because the data classification is "operational metadata" not "secret."' This is false; the script reads actual secrets.
findings.remediation: Remove this section entirely. Do not attempt to downplay or hide data collection activities.
